Distinguish a session from a meeting; de.question: A group of members meets on Tuesday evening to discuss club business and vote on new initiatives. The following week, the same group reconvenes to continue unfinished business from the previous meeting. What is the relationship between these two gatherings in parliamentary procedure?
options: Each gathering is a separate session, and together they form one meeting.
Both gatherings are part of a single session that spans two weeks.
The first gathering is a meeting; the second gathering is a session.
Each gathering is both a meeting and a session occurring simultaneously.
Distinguish a session from a meeting; de.explanation: In parliamentary procedure, a session is a series of meetings held under the same call to order, while a meeting is a single gathering. Two separate Tuesday gatherings without adjourning sine die (finally) are distinct meetings within one session. Understanding this distinction is foundational to recognizing when business can be continued versus when new orders of business must be called.

The meeting must be rescheduled for a date when all members can attend.
The members present may vote informally, though decisions are not binding.
Distinguish a session from a meeting; de.explanation: A quorum is the minimum number of members who must be present to conduct official business legally and bindingly. Without a quorum, no motions can be voted on or actions taken with parliamentary force. This protects against decisions made by a small faction and ensures representative decision-making.
Distinguish a session from a meeting; de.knowledge_area: Principles and Foundations of Parliamentary Law

Distinguish a session from a meeting; de.question: Parliamentary procedure rests on several foundational principles that guide how organizations conduct business. All of the following are fundamental principles of parliamentary law EXCEPT

While the majority rules, they do not have absolute power; minority protections ensure that smaller groups retain meaningful voice and due process. Seniority confers no parliamentary advantage in debate or voting rights.

Distinguish a session from a meeting; de.question: Parliamentary authority, bylaws, standing rules, and special rules of order all serve to govern how an organization operates. Which of the following correctly pairs a governance document with its proper function?

Distinguish a session from a meeting; de.explanation: Parliamentary authority (such as Robert's Rules of Order) is the foundational rulebook to which all other rules defer. Bylaws are permanent governing documents that require formal amendment; standing rules are permanent but may be suspended more easily; special rules of order apply for a defined period and supersede both but remain below parliamentary authority. The correct hierarchy ensures consistency and prevents local rules from contradicting established parliamentary principles.

Distinguish a session from a meeting; de.explanation: Quorum is required at the moment business is conducted, not merely at some point during the meeting. Classify any motion: main, subsidiary, p.question: A motion that can be made while another motion is pending and temporarily interrupts consideration of the main motion is classified as a ___.
options: privileged motion
bring-back motion
subsidiary motion
incidental motion
Classify any motion: main, subsidiary, p.explanation: Subsidiary motions are made while a main motion is pending and modify or dispose of the main motion without killing it entirely. They take precedence over the main motion but allow the assembly to return to it later. Privileged motions interrupt for more urgent business, while incidental motions address procedural questions that arise during debate.

Classify any motion: main, subsidiary, p.question: The system that ranks all standard motions from highest to lowest authority, determining which motions may be made when others are pending, is called the ___.
options: classification framework
authority ranking system
order of precedence
motion hierarchy structure
Classify any motion: main, subsidiary, p.explanation: The order of precedence is the formal parliamentary term for the ranked list of 13 standard motions, with privileged motions at the top and main motions at the bottom. This ranking determines which motions are in order (permissible) at any given moment during a meeting. The other options are descriptive but not the actual parliamentary term.

Classify any motion: main, subsidiary, p.question: Motions that address procedural questions and objections arising during debate—such as withdrawing a motion, requesting a point of order, or demanding a division of the question—are classified as ___.
options: subsidiary motions
incidental motions
main motions
privileged motions
Classify any motion: main, subsidiary, p.explanation: Incidental motions handle immediate procedural issues that pop up during consideration of other business and do not rank in the formal order of precedence the way privileged and subsidiary motions do. They must be decided before the assembly can continue with the main motion. Main motions introduce new business; subsidiary motions modify pending motions; and privileged motions address urgent assembly matters.

Classify any motion: main, subsidiary, p.question: A motion that can be made while another motion is pending and does not interrupt consideration of the main motion is classified as a ___.
options: bring-back motion
subsidiary motion
privileged motion
main motion
Classify any motion: main, subsidiary, p.explanation: Bring-back motions allow an assembly to reconsider or take up again a motion that was previously decided, without interrupting the current main motion. These are distinct from privileged motions, which do interrupt consideration, and from subsidiary motions, which modify or affect the main motion directly. This classification depends on understanding how different motion types interact with pending business.

Classify any motion: main, subsidiary, p.question: Which of the following motions would all be classified as privileged motions?
options: Amend, divide the question, and object to consideration
Withdraw a motion, call for orders of the day, and point of order
Postpone indefinitely, lay on the table, and previous question
Adjourn, recess, and raise a question of privilege
Classify any motion: main, subsidiary, p.explanation: Privileged motions are high-ranking motions that can interrupt consideration of the main motion because they address urgent assembly business or member rights. Adjourn, recess, and raise a question of privilege are all classic examples that do not relate to the pending main motion but require immediate attention. The other bundles mix incidental motions (withdraw, point of order), subsidiary motions (amend, postpone indefinitely, previous question), or distract with procedural terms that are not all motions.

Classify any motion: main, subsidiary, p.question: A motion that can be made while another motion is pending and interrupts consideration of that motion to address an urgent matter concerning the assembly itself is classified as a(n) ___.
options: subsidiary motion
privileged motion
main motion
incidental motion
Classify any motion: main, subsidiary, p.explanation: Privileged motions rank highest in the order of precedence and may interrupt consideration of any other motion because they address urgent assembly business such as adjournment or addressing a point of privilege. Unlike incidental motions (which handle procedural objections) or subsidiary motions (which modify pending business), privileged motions take immediate precedence.

Classify any motion: main, subsidiary, p.question: Which of the following motions could all be classified as motions that address procedural questions and objections arising during debate?
options: Adjourn, recess, and raise a question of privilege
Table the motion, limit debate, and move the previous question
Point of order, appeal, and withdraw the motion
Amend, refer to committee, and lay on the table
Classify any motion: main, subsidiary, p.explanation: Point of order, appeal, and withdraw the motion are all incidental motions—they handle procedural objections and questions that arise during debate. The first option lists privileged motions; the second and third options mix subsidiary motions with incidental ones.

Classify any motion: main, subsidiary, p.question: When determining motion classification, a motion is NOT classified as privileged if it ___.
options: directly modifies or affects the treatment of the main motion
takes precedence over most other motions in the order of precedence
can be made while another motion is pending without interrupting consideration of that motion
addresses an urgent matter concerning the assembly itself
Classify any motion: main, subsidiary, p.explanation: Privileged motions interrupt pending business to address urgent assembly matters (like adjournment or a question of privilege). A motion that directly modifies the main motion is a subsidiary motion, not a privileged motion. The other options are all characteristics of privileged motions.

Classify any motion: main, subsidiary, p.question: Which of the following motions would all be ranked high in the order of precedence due to their urgent nature concerning the assembly?
options: Amend, divide the question, and limit debate
Postpone indefinitely, postpone to a certain time, and table
Withdraw the motion, point of order, and appeal
Adjourn, recess, and fix the time to which to adjourn
Classify any motion: main, subsidiary, p.explanation: Privileged motions like Adjourn, Recess, and Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn rank highest in precedence because they address urgent matters affecting the assembly itself and can interrupt consideration of pending business. The other options include subsidiary motions (Postpone, Table, Amend) and incidental motions (Withdraw, Point of Order, Appeal), which have lower precedence rankings.

Classify any motion: main, subsidiary, p.question: All of the following are characteristics of main motions EXCEPT
options: They bring new business before the assembly for consideration
They can be made while another motion is temporarily interrupting debate
They form the foundation of parliamentary procedure by introducing substantive matters
They can be made only when no other motion is pending
Classify any motion: main, subsidiary, p.explanation: Main motions are the only motions that introduce new business and can be made only when no other motion is pending; they cannot be made while another motion is interrupting consideration. The other three options all accurately describe properties of main motions that distinguish them from subsidiary, privileged, and incidental motions.

Apply the 13-motion order of precedence.question: A motion that can be made while another motion is pending and takes precedence over it is called a(n) ___
options: main motion
subsidiary motion
incidental motion
privileged motion
Apply the 13-motion order of precedence.explanation: Privileged motions have the highest precedence and can interrupt pending business because they address urgent assembly needs. Main motions cannot be made when another motion is pending, and subsidiary and incidental motions do not take precedence over other motions.

Apply the 13-motion order of precedence.question: The process of grouping motions into five broad categories based on their function and precedence rules is called ___
options: motion hierarchy
precedence ordering
classifying motions
ranking motions
Apply the 13-motion order of precedence.explanation: Classification is the foundational skill that sorts all motions into main, subsidiary, privileged, incidental, and bring-back categories. Once classified, each category follows specific precedence rules. Ranking refers to the numerical order within the full 13-motion sequence, not the broader categorical grouping.

Apply the 13-motion order of precedence.question: A motion that can only be made when no other motion is pending and serves as the original business before the assembly is called a(n) ___
options: main motion
subsidiary motion
incidental motion
privileged motion
Apply the 13-motion order of precedence.explanation: A main motion introduces the primary business of the assembly and cannot be made while another motion is pending. Privileged motions interrupt pending business, subsidiary motions modify other motions, and incidental motions address procedural questions—none of these can serve as the original, standalone business.

Apply the 13-motion order of precedence.question: A motion that can be made while another motion is pending, does not require a second, and cannot be debated or amended is classified as a(n) ___
options: bring-back motion
subsidiary motion
main motion
incidental motion
Apply the 13-motion order of precedence.explanation: Incidental motions are characterized by their ability to be made while other business is pending, their lack of requirement for a second, and their non-debatable, non-amendable nature. They address procedural questions that arise during a meeting, such as points of order or appeals. Subsidiary motions require a pending motion and do require a second, while main motions are made when no other motion is pending.

Apply the 13-motion order of precedence.question: A motion that can be made while another motion is pending and serves to modify or dispose of the pending motion without voting on it directly is classified as a(n) ___
options: subsidiary motion
main motion
bring-back motion
privileged motion
Apply the 13-motion order of precedence.explanation: Subsidiary motions function to modify, delay, or dispose of the pending main motion and rank below the main motion in precedence. They include motions like amend, postpone, and table, all of which address the pending business without immediately voting on it.

Apply the 13-motion order of precedence.question: The five broad categories into which all parliamentary motions are grouped based on their function and ranking are main, subsidiary, privileged, incidental, and ___
options: emergency motions
unanimous consent motions
bring-back motions
procedural motions
Apply the 13-motion order of precedence.explanation: Bring-back motions (such as reconsider and rescind) form the fifth category and allow an assembly to revisit or undo previous decisions. Together with the other four categories, they complete the classification system used to determine which motions are in order at any given time.

Apply the 13-motion order of precedence.question: Which of the following motions would be in order if a main motion and an amendment to that main motion are currently pending?
options: A motion to reconsider a vote taken earlier in the meeting
A motion to adjourn the assembly
A motion to table the amendment only, leaving the main motion pending
A motion to postpone the main motion to a later time
Apply the 13-motion order of precedence.explanation: A motion to adjourn is a privileged motion with the highest rank in the order of precedence and can be made at almost any time, including when multiple motions are pending. The other options either rank lower than the pending amendment (postpone, table) or cannot be made until a vote has occurred (reconsider), making them out of order in this situation.

Apply the 13-motion order of precedence.question: Which of the following motions would have the HIGHEST precedence and be in order even if a main motion, an amendment, and a motion to postpone indefinitely are all currently pending?
options: A motion to lay the pending questions on the table
A motion to refer the main motion to a committee
A motion to call for a recess of the assembly
A motion to amend the amendment
Apply the 13-motion order of precedence.explanation: A motion to recess is a privileged motion with the highest precedence in parliamentary procedure because it addresses an urgent need affecting the assembly itself and cannot be delayed by other pending business. Motions to amend, postpone, or table are all subsidiary motions with lower precedence and could not interrupt a privileged motion. Understanding the 13-motion order of precedence requires knowing that privileged motions sit at the top of the ranking.

Apply the 13-motion order of precedence.question: A motion that can be made while another motion is pending, requires a second, can be debated, and is used to change the wording or scope of the pending motion is classified as a(n) ___
options: amendment
point of order
motion to table
motion to postpone
Apply the 13-motion order of precedence.explanation: An amendment is a subsidiary motion that modifies the pending motion's wording or scope while debate and a second are required. It is one of the most commonly used motions to refine business before voting, and understanding amendments is essential to applying precedence rules correctly.

Apply the 13-motion order of precedence.question: The strict ranking system that determines which motions can interrupt pending motions and which cannot is called the ___.
options: motion classification hierarchy
motion interrupt priority
parliamentary ranking protocol
order of precedence
Apply the 13-motion order of precedence.explanation: The order of precedence is the 13-motion ranking system that establishes which motions take priority over others and when they may be introduced. This is a core concept in parliamentary procedure that allows an assembly to manage competing motions fairly and systematically.

For any motion, state: second required?.question: Which of the following combinations correctly describes the parliamentary characteristics of a main motion?
options: Does not require a second, is not debatable, is amendable, requires a unanimous vote, cannot be reconsidered
Requires a second, is debatable, is amendable, requires a majority vote, can be reconsidered
Requires a second, is debatable, is not amendable, requires a majority vote, can be reconsidered
Requires a second, is not debatable, is amendable, requires a two-thirds vote, can be reconsidered
For any motion, state: second required?.explanation: A main motion—the primary business brought before an assembly—requires a second to ensure support, allows full debate on its merits, can be modified through amendments to improve it, passes by simple majority vote in most cases, and can be reconsidered if new facts emerge. This standard set of rules applies to most ordinary business motions. Understanding these five characteristics is essential for recognizing how a main motion differs from subsidiary or privileged motions.

For any motion, state: second required?.question: A motion that is debatable allows the assembly to discuss its merits before voting. Which of the following motions is NOT debatable?
options: Main motion
Postpone indefinitely
Amend
Point of order
For any motion, state: second required?.explanation: A point of order is a procedural device used to raise an objection about a rule violation and is not debatable—it must be ruled on immediately by the chair. In contrast, main motions, amendments, and postponement motions all allow for debate on their substance before the assembly votes.

For any motion, state: second required?.question: The ability to reconsider a motion refers to ___
options: the chair's power to reject a motion without debate
amending a motion after it has been passed into effect
reopening and revoting on a motion that has already been decided
appealing a ruling by requesting a formal committee review
For any motion, state: second required?.explanation: Reconsideration allows the assembly to bring back a previously voted motion for another discussion and vote, typically if new information emerges or circumstances change. Not all motions are reconsidera­ble; some procedural rules lock certain decisions in place to maintain stability.

For any motion, state: second required?.question: A motion that requires a second must have another member formally support it before the assembly considers it. Which of the following statements best completes this concept: A motion is considered properly introduced when ___
options: it receives a majority vote from the general membership
the member obtains a second and the chair states the motion to the assembly
the chair recognizes the member and the motion is stated aloud
a second is obtained and the motion is placed on the agenda for debate
For any motion, state: second required?.explanation: A motion requires both a second and official statement by the chair to be properly before the assembly. The combination of obtaining a second and having the chair formally state the motion ensures the assembly has formally received and can now consider the motion.

For any motion, state: second required?.question: Parliamentary procedure distinguishes motions by their characteristics. A motion that is amendable means the assembly has the power to ___
options: modify or change its specific terms before voting on it
appeal the chair's interpretation of parliamentary law to the full membership
postpone it to a later meeting without losing the right to reconsider
demand a recount of votes cast after the chair announces the result
For any motion, state: second required?.explanation: An amendable motion is one that the assembly can alter or modify by changing its wording or specific provisions before taking a vote. This allows members to adjust the motion's terms to better suit the assembly's intent without abandoning the motion entirely.

For any motion, state: second required?.question: A motion that requires a second must have another member formally support it before discussion can proceed. Which of the following motions requires a second?
options: Request to withdraw a motion
Main motion
Point of order
Parliamentary inquiry
For any motion, state: second required?.explanation: A main motion requires a second before it can be discussed or voted on by the assembly. Points of order, parliamentary inquiries, and requests to withdraw motions do not require a second because they are procedural or urgent in nature. This tests understanding of which motions trigger the formal support requirement.

For any motion, state: second required?.question: The characteristic of being amendable means a motion allows the assembly to ___
options: modify or change its specific terms before the final vote
postpone it indefinitely without a vote
table it for later discussion without a time limit
reconsider it after the vote has been taken
For any motion, state: second required?.explanation: An amendable motion permits members to propose changes to its wording or terms before the assembly votes on it. This is distinct from reconsidering (voting again after a decision) or postponing (delaying without modification). Understanding amendability is core to parliamentary motion characteristics.

For any motion, state: second required?.question: A motion is considered debatable when the assembly has the power to ___
options: modify its wording before voting
request that it be placed in writing by the secretary
discuss its merits and reasoning before taking a vote
postpone action on it to a future meeting
For any motion, state: second required?.explanation: A debatable motion allows members to speak for or against it and present arguments about its merits before the vote occurs. Debatability focuses on discussion and reasoning, not on modification (amendability), postponement, or recording procedures. This distinguishes debatable motions from those that proceed directly to a vote.

For any motion, state: second required?.question: Which of the following statements about motion characteristics is correct?
options: All motions are debatable, and all motions require a second before being considered
If a motion requires a second, it must also be debatable and amendable
Motions that cannot be amended are always decided by a two-thirds vote rather than majority
A motion can be both amendable and non-debatable, depending on its type and purpose
For any motion, state: second required?.explanation: Different motions have different combinations of characteristics; some are debatable while others are not, and some are amendable while others are restricted. For example, a point of order is neither debatable nor amendable, while a main motion is both. The key is that the five characteristics (second, debatable, amendable, vote type, reconsiderable) are independent variables that combine differently for each motion type.

For any motion, state: second required?.question: All of the following motions can be reconsidered EXCEPT
options: Refer to committee
Amend
Postpone indefinitely
Adjournment
For any motion, state: second required?.explanation: Adjournment cannot be reconsidered because once a meeting is adjourned, the assembly has formally ended and cannot revisit that decision. Postpone indefinitely, refer to committee, and amend are all motions whose outcomes can be reconsidered if the assembly chooses to do so. This tests understanding of which motions fall outside the reconsideration rule.

Identify the purpose and correct use of.question: A motion that brings a previously tabled matter back before the assembly for reconsideration is called a(n) ___
options: motion to reconsider
motion to lay on the table
motion to take from the table
motion to postpone
Identify the purpose and correct use of.explanation: The motion to take from the table is specifically used to remove a previously tabled motion from the table and restore it to the assembly's consideration. This motion allows the assembly to revisit business that was set aside earlier. The other options serve different purposes: postpone delays action to a specific time, reconsider allows voting on a motion again immediately after, and lay on the table temporarily sets aside pending business.

Identify the purpose and correct use of.question: The process of delaying action on a motion until a specified future time is accomplished through ___
options: moving to table
moving to postpone
moving to amend
calling the question
Identify the purpose and correct use of.explanation: The motion to postpone defers consideration of a motion to a definite time in the future, allowing the assembly to address more pressing matters. This differs from tabling, which temporarily sets business aside without a specific time to resume, and calling the question, which ends debate and forces a vote. Amending modifies the wording of a motion rather than delaying it.

Identify the purpose and correct use of.question: Which of the following statements correctly describes the characteristics of a motion to adjourn?
options: It is not debatable, requires a second, cannot be amended, and needs only a majority vote
It is debatable, requires a second, cannot be amended, and needs only a majority vote
It is not debatable, requires a second, can be amended, and needs a majority vote
It is debatable, does not require a second, can be amended, and needs a two-thirds vote
Identify the purpose and correct use of.explanation: The motion to adjourn ends the meeting and is not debatable because it would delay the assembly's departure. It requires a second to proceed, cannot be amended because adding conditions would change its essential purpose, and only needs a majority vote to pass. The other options incorrectly combine these characteristics with those of other motions like postpone or table.

Identify the purpose and correct use of.question: Which of the following correctly pairs a motion with its primary purpose?
options: Motion to appeal—directs the assembly to vote immediately without further debate on a pending motion
Motion to lay on the table—postpones action until a specific date agreed upon by the assembly
Motion to divide the question—ends debate and forces immediate consideration of an unrelated motion
Motion to reconsider—permits voting again immediately after a motion is defeated or passed, within the same session
Identify the purpose and correct use of.explanation: The motion to reconsider allows a member who voted with the prevailing side to request a new vote on a motion during the same meeting, permitting the assembly to correct a decision before adjourning. This is its defining purpose and primary use. The other pairings are incorrect: appeal challenges a chair's ruling, lay on the table sets business aside temporarily without a fixed return time, and divide the question separates a complex motion into parts for individual voting.

Identify the purpose and correct use of.question: All of the processes and procedures used to guide group decision-making and maintain order during a meeting make up ___
options: parliamentary procedure
organizational governance
meeting management
assembly protocol
Identify the purpose and correct use of.explanation: Parliamentary procedure is the formal system of rules and processes that enables groups to conduct orderly meetings, make decisions fairly, and handle motions efficiently. While the other options relate to meetings broadly, only parliamentary procedure specifically names the disciplined set of rules designed to guide deliberation and maintain order during assembly proceedings.

Identify the purpose and correct use of.question: A motion that requires a second before it can be debated or voted on is known as ___.
options: an incidental motion
a subsidiary motion
a main motion
a privileged motion
Identify the purpose and correct use of.explanation: A main motion is the fundamental type of motion that introduces a new item of business and always requires a second to proceed. All other motion types have different procedural requirements; for example, privileged motions do not require a second, while incidental motions address procedural questions.

Identify the purpose and correct use of.question: A motion to amend serves to ___.
options: end debate and force an immediate vote on the question
modify the wording or terms of a pending motion
refer the pending motion to a committee for further study
delay consideration of a main motion until a future meeting
Identify the purpose and correct use of.explanation: A motion to amend is a subsidiary motion that allows members to propose changes to the wording, terms, or scope of a motion under consideration before it is voted on. The other options describe postponement, closing debate, and referral to committee—each a distinct motion with its own purpose.

Identify the purpose and correct use of.question: The primary purpose of using a motion to limit or extend debate is to ___.
options: eliminate the need for a second before debate begins
allow the chair to cast a deciding vote without discussion
prevent any member from speaking more than once on a motion
control the length and scope of discussion on a pending motion
Identify the purpose and correct use of.explanation: A motion to limit or extend debate serves to manage how much discussion occurs on a motion—either shortening the debate period or lengthening it beyond normal rules. This preserves order while ensuring adequate consideration of the pending business.

Identify the purpose and correct use of.question: Which of the following statements correctly describes the characteristics that apply to a motion to postpone to a definite time?
options: It is not debatable, does not require a second, and cannot be amended under any circumstances
It requires a second, is not debatable, cannot be amended, and needs only a plurality vote
It requires a second, is debatable, may be amended, and needs a majority vote to pass
It does not require a second, is debatable, cannot be amended, and needs a two-thirds vote
Identify the purpose and correct use of.explanation: A motion to postpone to a definite time is a subsidiary motion that requires a second, is debatable (members may discuss when the motion should be postponed to), can be amended (the time itself can be changed), and passes by a simple majority vote. These characteristics allow the assembly flexibility in scheduling consideration of pending business.

Identify the purpose and correct use of.question: A motion that halts all discussion and brings an immediate vote on the pending question is called a(n) ___.
options: motion for the previous question
motion to postpone indefinitely
motion to limit debate
motion to lay on the table
Identify the purpose and correct use of.explanation: The motion for the previous question (also called 'call the question') is a high-privilege motion designed to stop debate immediately and force a vote on the pending motion. It is not debatable and requires a two-thirds majority to pass. This motion is distinct from limiting debate, which extends or shortens discussion time without ending it entirely.

List the six steps in handling a main mo.question: In parliamentary procedure, the first step in handling a main motion is to ___.
options: amend the motion before discussion
make the motion and have it seconded
debate the motion on the floor
call for a vote immediately
List the six steps in handling a main mo.explanation: Making and seconding a motion is the foundational first step that must occur before any other action can take place in the handling process. Without this step, there is no motion to debate, amend, or vote on. This establishes the motion as properly before the assembly.

List the six steps in handling a main mo.question: The process of formally proposing an action to an assembly, obtaining a second, discussing it, possibly changing it, and then voting on it represents ___.
options: the amendment procedure
the debate framework
the handling of a main motion
the rules of recognition
List the six steps in handling a main mo.explanation: This sequence of steps—proposal, second, discussion, amendment, and vote—defines the complete main motion process. While amendments, debate rules, and recognition are all components or related concepts, the umbrella term for this entire structured process is 'handling a main motion.' This is the core business procedure in parliamentary meetings.

List the six steps in handling a main mo.question: Which of the following would ALL be appropriate steps that occur during the handling of a main motion?
options: Making the motion, tabling it indefinitely, and adjourning the meeting without a vote
Announcing the result of the vote, reopening debate after the vote closes, and seconding the motion again
Obtaining recognition from the chair, proposing an amendment, and skipping debate entirely
Seconding the motion, debating its merits, and calling for a vote on the final version
List the six steps in handling a main mo.explanation: Seconding, debating, and voting represent three sequential, legitimate steps in the standard motion-handling sequence. The other options contain actions that either skip essential steps, occur out of order, or violate proper procedure—such as tabling without debate, reopening debate after the vote is final, or seconding after the motion has already been made and seconded.

List the six steps in handling a main mo.question: After a motion has been made and seconded, the next step in handling a main motion typically involves ___.
options: allowing the assembly to debate and discuss the motion
automatically amending it to reflect majority preference
sending the motion to a committee for review
calling for a vote without discussion
List the six steps in handling a main mo.explanation: Once a motion is properly made and seconded, the assembly moves to the debate phase, where members discuss the merits and implications of the proposal. This discussion period is a standard and important step before amendments are considered or a vote is called. Voting without debate, automatic amendments, and committee referral are not the normal third step in the sequence.

List the six steps in handling a main mo.question: In parliamentary procedure, after a main motion has been made and seconded, what is the third step in the formal handling process?
options: Members submit written amendments to the chair for review
The chair states the question to the assembly for debate and consideration
The chair calls for a vote immediately without discussion
The motion is recorded in the minutes before any discussion occurs
List the six steps in handling a main mo.explanation: After a motion is made and seconded, the chair must state the question to formally present it to the assembly, allowing members to understand what is being debated. This step ensures the assembly knows exactly what motion is under consideration before debate begins. The other options either skip the debate process entirely or occur at different points in the handling sequence.

List the six steps in handling a main mo.question: All of the following are actual steps in the six-step process of handling a main motion EXCEPT
options: the chair restates the motion for final clarity before the vote
the presiding officer appoints a committee to decide the motion's merit
a member obtains recognition from the chair to speak during debate
the assembly votes on whether to accept or reject the motion
List the six steps in handling a main mo.explanation: Appointing a committee to decide a motion's merit is not a standard step in handling a main motion; the assembly itself decides through debate and vote. The other three—restating before voting, member recognition during debate, and the assembly's vote—are genuine procedural steps that occur during proper motion handling.

List the six steps in handling a main mo.question: A member wishes to suggest a change to the wording of a pending main motion to make it more acceptable to the assembly. What action should the member take?
options: Propose an amendment to the motion
Withdraw the original motion entirely and start over
Ask for a recount of the assembly members present
Request the chair postpone the vote indefinitely
List the six steps in handling a main mo.explanation: An amendment is the formal parliamentary tool for modifying the wording or scope of a pending motion while keeping the original business before the assembly. This allows members to improve a motion without scrapping it entirely, which is why amendments are a key part of the motion-handling process.

List the six steps in handling a main mo.question: Which of the following would ALL occur after a main motion has been seconded?
options: The chair recognizes the member and the member makes the motion
The assembly votes immediately without any discussion or amendments
Another member seconds the motion and the chair announces it
The chair states the question, members debate, and a vote is called
List the six steps in handling a main mo.explanation: After a main motion is seconded, the chair formally states the question to open debate, members discuss its merits, and eventually a vote is called. The other options either describe steps that come before the seconding or violate parliamentary procedure by skipping necessary steps.

List the six steps in handling a main mo.question: In parliamentary procedure, the fourth step in handling a main motion involves the chair performing which action?
options: Declaring the motion adopted or defeated based on the assembly's vote
Closing debate and announcing that a vote will now be taken
Stating the question to formally present the motion for debate and consideration
Requesting a second to validate that the motion has assembly support
List the six steps in handling a main mo.explanation: After a motion is made and seconded, the chair states the question, which formally presents the motion to the assembly for discussion and debate. This is a distinct step from calling for the vote (which comes later) and is the chair's responsibility to clearly articulate what the assembly will be considering. This step ensures all members understand exactly what motion is before them before debate begins.

List the six steps in handling a main mo.question: Which of the following would ALL represent acceptable actions a member could take to modify a main motion that is currently under debate?
options: Request that the motion be withdrawn entirely, or move to table the motion until the next meeting
Offer a primary amendment to change specific words, or propose a secondary amendment to modify the primary amendment
Request a roll-call vote on the original motion, or ask the chair to restate the question for clarity
Propose that the motion be sent to committee for study, or demand an immediate vote without further debate
List the six steps in handling a main mo.explanation: Primary amendments (first-degree amendments) directly change the language of the main motion, while secondary amendments (second-degree amendments) modify a primary amendment. Both are legitimate ways members can adjust a motion during the handling process. The other options represent different parliamentary actions—withdrawal, tabling, voting procedures, and committee referral—but are not modification tools for refining the motion's actual wording during debate.

Apply the rules of debate and recognitio.question: The period during which a member is actively speaking on a motion and presenting arguments for or against it is called ___
options: amendment
adjournment
recognition
debate
Apply the rules of debate and recognitio.explanation: Debate is the formal discussion period where members speak to present arguments supporting or opposing a motion. Recognition is the process of being acknowledged to speak, amendment is a modification to a motion, and adjournment ends the meeting—none of these are the speaking period itself.

Apply the rules of debate and recognitio.question: Before a member can present arguments during debate, they must first obtain ___
options: approval from the secretary
a second to their motion
recognition from the chair
written consent from the treasurer
Apply the rules of debate and recognitio.explanation: Recognition from the chair is the formal acknowledgment that allows a member to speak during debate. Without recognition, a member cannot participate in debate, even if their motion has been seconded. A second is needed for the motion itself, not for speaking rights, and secretary or treasurer approval is not required for debate participation.

Apply the rules of debate and recognitio.question: The process of modifying or improving a motion by changing its wording or content is known as ___
options: amendment
postponement
tabling
division
Apply the rules of debate and recognitio.explanation: Amendment is the formal procedure for changing the wording or substance of a motion before a vote. Postponement delays consideration, tabling sets it aside temporarily, and division requests a count of votes—none of these modify the motion's content itself.

Apply the rules of debate and recognitio.question: Which of the following would ALL be required steps before debate on a main motion can begin?
options: The motion must be printed AND distributed to all members before the meeting
The motion must be amended AND the secretary must record it in the minutes
The motion must be seconded AND the chair must state the question
The chair must recognize the maker AND a third member must support it
Apply the rules of debate and recognitio.explanation: Both a second and the chair's statement of the question are necessary prerequisites to open debate. The second validates that more than one member supports discussion, and the chair's formal statement of the question confirms it has been properly introduced. Amendments may come during debate (not before it must begin), recording in minutes happens after decisions, and printing/distribution are not parliamentary requirements for debate to start.

Apply the rules of debate and recognitio.question: The recognition and debate process is governed by all of the following EXCEPT
options: the requirement that members must be physically present in the room
the specific time limit allocated for each member's remarks
the rule that only one member speaks at a time
the priority order in which members may speak
Apply the rules of debate and recognitio.explanation: While parliamentary procedure requires orderly recognition and one speaker at a time, it does not mandate that members be physically present in the room to participate in debate—some organizations permit remote or proxy participation. Priority order, time limits per speaker, and the one-at-a-time rule are all core controls on debate to maintain order and fairness.

Apply the rules of debate and recognitio.question: Which of the following would ALL be appropriate steps in the debate process once a motion has been properly stated by the chair?
options: Debate is closed immediately, all members vote at once, and the result is recorded without discussion
The chair automatically votes, members submit written comments, and the motion is filed
Members nominate replacements for the chair, the motion is postponed indefinitely, and a new motion replaces it
Members seek recognition, speak in turn, and present arguments either for or against the motion
Apply the rules of debate and recognitio.explanation: Once a motion is properly stated by the chair, members who wish to speak must seek recognition from the chair, are granted the floor in turn, and may present arguments supporting or opposing the motion. This orderly process allows all viewpoints to be heard before a vote is taken.

Apply the rules of debate and recognitio.question: A motion that is brought before the assembly for the first time and has not yet been assigned to a committee is called a ___
options: subsidiary motion
main motion
privileged motion
incidental motion
Apply the rules of debate and recognitio.explanation: A main motion is the primary motion that introduces new business to the assembly and serves as the basis for debate and action. It is distinct from privileged, subsidiary, and incidental motions, which all relate to handling the main motion itself or managing procedure.

Apply the rules of debate and recognitio.question: When a member wishes to speak during debate but has not yet obtained the floor, they must ___
options: submit their remarks in writing to the secretary
stand at a podium and begin speaking immediately
address the chair and request recognition to speak
raise their hand and wait to be assigned a turn by the chair
Apply the rules of debate and recognitio.explanation: Seeking recognition means formally addressing the chair to request permission to speak. This is a critical procedural step that ensures the chair controls the order of speakers and maintains orderly debate. Simply raising a hand or standing without addressing the chair does not constitute proper recognition.

Apply the rules of debate and recognitio.question: The process of controlling the order in which members speak during debate, ensuring that speakers are heard one at a time according to parliamentary rules, is known as ___
options: speaking order
recognition protocol
floor management
debate sequencing
Apply the rules of debate and recognitio.explanation: Recognition protocol (or simply recognition) refers to the chair's authority to grant members permission to speak in an orderly fashion during debate. The chair maintains control of who may speak and in what order, preventing multiple members from speaking simultaneously and ensuring fair, organized debate. This is a fundamental rule of orderly assembly procedure.

Apply the rules of debate and recognitio.question: All of the following are appropriate speaking practices during parliamentary debate EXCEPT
options: Directing all remarks to other members in the assembly to persuade them
Addressing the chair by their proper title before presenting arguments
Speaking directly to the chair rather than to other members
Using respectful language and avoiding personal attacks on other speakers
Apply the rules of debate and recognitio.explanation: During parliamentary debate, speakers must address the chair, not other members directly. All other options reflect proper debate conduct—speakers use respectful language, avoid personal attacks, and maintain parliamentary courtesy by speaking through the chair as the presiding officer.

Apply amendment rules, including degrees.question: A change or modification proposed to a motion during debate is called a(n) ___.
options: postponement
substitution
revision
amendment
Apply amendment rules, including degrees.explanation: An amendment is the formal term for any proposed change to the wording or intent of a motion while it is under consideration. This is distinct from other motions like substitution (which replaces the entire motion) or postponement (which delays it). Knowing this term is essential to understanding how parliamentary procedure manages debate and modifications.

Apply amendment rules, including degrees.question: The process by which proposed changes to a motion are formally considered and voted on by the assembly is part of which broader parliamentary function?
options: Processing business and handling motions
Establishing quorum and verifying attendance
Electing officers and filling vacancies
Adopting standing rules and bylaws
Apply amendment rules, including degrees.explanation: Processing business encompasses all the steps required to handle motions, including presenting them, debating them, amending them, and voting on them. This is the core parliamentary function that allows an organization to conduct its deliberative work.

Apply amendment rules, including degrees.question: When an amendment is proposed to a main motion, the assembly must first determine the amendment's degree. A first-degree amendment is one that is proposed directly to the ___.
options: presiding officer's ruling
original main motion
second-degree amendment
committee recommendation
Apply amendment rules, including degrees.explanation: A first-degree amendment modifies the original main motion directly. Understanding degrees of amendment is foundational to applying amendment rules—a second-degree amendment would modify a first-degree amendment, not the main motion itself.

Apply amendment rules, including degrees.question: Which of the following would all be examples of valid first-degree amendments to a main motion?
options: approving the motion as stated, amending an existing amendment, and rejecting the speaker
tabling the motion, postponing it indefinitely, and referring it to committee
dividing the motion, calling for a recount, and challenging the chair's ruling
striking out a word, inserting a phrase, and substituting an entire paragraph
Apply amendment rules, including degrees.explanation: First-degree amendments directly modify the original main motion through specific actions such as striking (removing) words, inserting new text, or substituting sections. These are all forms of alteration applied directly to the motion itself, not procedural motions or amendments to other amendments.

Apply amendment rules, including degrees.question: When a first-degree amendment has been adopted by the assembly, any subsequent change proposed to that amendment is called a(n) ___ amendment.
options: tertiary
subsidiary
second-degree
primary
Apply amendment rules, including degrees.explanation: A second-degree amendment is a proposed change to a first-degree amendment that has already been accepted by the assembly. This creates a hierarchy of amendments: the original motion is modified by the first-degree amendment, and then that first-degree amendment can itself be modified by a second-degree amendment. Understanding amendment degrees is essential to applying amendment rules correctly during debate.

Apply amendment rules, including degrees.question: Which of the following would all represent valid forms that a first-degree amendment might take?
options: Creating a subsidiary motion, filing a point of order, or requesting unanimous consent
Sending the motion to committee, limiting debate time, or extending the session
Adding language to clarify intent, removing specific words, or restating an entire clause
Postponing the vote, withdrawing the motion, or calling the question
Apply amendment rules, including degrees.explanation: Valid forms of a first-degree amendment modify the original motion itself—by adding language, striking words, or rewriting portions. The other options describe parliamentary procedures or motions that are distinct from amendments. Understanding the specific forms amendments can take is essential to applying amendment rules correctly.

Apply amendment rules, including degrees.question: When a second-degree amendment has been adopted, any further change proposed to it is classified as a(n) ___ amendment.
options: hostile
primary
subsidiary
third-degree
Apply amendment rules, including degrees.explanation: A third-degree amendment is a proposed change to a second-degree amendment. This follows the hierarchy of amendment degrees: first-degree amendments modify the main motion, second-degree amendments modify first-degree amendments, and third-degree amendments modify second-degree amendments. Understanding this three-level structure is essential to applying amendment rules correctly.
