This is distinct from putting the question, which occurs later when the chair takes the vote. Recognition of the floor and seconding are earlier steps in the motion process.

Reading and approval of the minutes.question: According to parliamentary procedure debate rules, all of the following are true EXCEPT:
options: Members may attack the motives of other speakers to strengthen their position on the motion
A member may speak up to two times per motion per day, with a ten-minute limit each
Only the member recognized by the chair may speak during debate
The maker of the motion has the right to speak first but may not speak against their own motion
Reading and approval of the minutes.explanation: Attacking the motives of other members is explicitly prohibited in parliamentary debate; speakers must keep remarks germane, address the chair rather than other members, and refer to speakers impersonally (not by name). This rule maintains decorum and keeps focus on the motion itself, not on personal attacks. The other three options correctly describe actual debate rules.

Reading and approval of the minutes.question: Which of the following accurately describes when the chair must relinquish control and cannot participate in debate?
options: When the debate concerns a special order that was set at the previous meeting
When the presiding officer wishes to speak in favor of or against a motion under consideration
When a member calls for a vote by ballot instead of voice vote
When more than ten members have requested the floor during the same debate period
Reading and approval of the minutes.explanation: To remain impartial, the presiding officer must turn over the chair to another member if they want to participate in debate; they cannot debate while presiding. The chair only resumes their role after the question has been decided. This rule preserves the chair's neutrality and prevents the person controlling speaking order from favoring their own position.

Reading and approval of the minutes.question: A member of the assembly seeks recognition from the chair to present a motion. Before the motion can officially belong to the assembly and be debated, what specific action by another member must occur?
options: Another member must second the motion
The chair must announce the vote on the motion
All members must indicate their intent to participate in debate
The member must make a formal written proposal in the minutes
Reading and approval of the minutes.explanation: According to parliamentary procedure, a second is required to show that at least two members want the motion considered before the chair can state the question and open it for debate. A motion without a second is simply not taken up by the assembly. This step is fundamental to the six-step motion process and occurs before the chair formally states the question.

Reading and approval of the minutes.question: The process by which the chair formally recognizes that a motion has been both made and supported, announces it to the assembly in a standard form, and thereby makes it the official pending business is called ___
options: Opening debate
Putting the question
Seconding the motion
Recording in the minutes
Reading and approval of the minutes.explanation: Putting the question is the chair's act of formally stating the motion in the standard form ('It is moved and seconded that...'), which transforms it from a member's proposal into official business of the assembly ready for debate. This is distinct from seconding (a member's action) and from opening debate (which follows). Only after the chair puts the question does the motion officially belong to the assembly.

Reading and approval of the minutes.question: In parliamentary procedure, the formal permission given by the chair to a member to speak is called having the ___.
options: consent
recognition
floor
motion
Reading and approval of the minutes.explanation: Having 'the floor' means the chair has recognized the member and given them permission to speak. This is a foundational term in parliamentary procedure, distinct from 'recognition' (the act the chair performs) and 'consent' (a different procedural mechanism).

Reading and approval of the minutes.question: The proper form for a member to introduce a motion is to say ___.
options: I so move that the assembly adopt this proposal
I move that the assembly consider the following
I make a motion that we proceed as follows
I move that we take the following action
Reading and approval of the minutes.explanation: Parliamentary procedure requires the exact phrasing 'I move that ...' to properly state a motion. The other options use incorrect forms such as 'I so move,' 'I make a motion,' or 'I move that we consider'—improper variations that do not meet RONR standards.

Reading and approval of the minutes.question: Which of the following would all be proper voting methods under RONR?
options: Rising vote, show of hands, and roll call vote
Voice vote, proxy vote, and unanimous consent vote
Ballot vote, absentee vote, and general consent vote
Voice vote, rising vote, and ballot vote
Reading and approval of the minutes.explanation: Voice vote (viva voce), rising vote, show of hands, ballot vote, and roll call vote are all standard voting methods under RONR. Proxy voting and absentee voting are only permitted if bylaws specifically authorize them, and unanimous/general consent is not itself a voting method but rather a consent procedure that can be overridden by a single objection.

Reading and approval of the minutes.question: A motion requires a second before the chair states the question EXCEPT when ___.
options: the motion is made by the presiding officer
the motion is offered during new business
the motion is reported by a standing committee
the motion addresses a matter of immediate importance
Reading and approval of the minutes.explanation: Committee-reported motions require no second, because the committee itself represents group consensus and support. All other main motions require a second from another member to show that at least two members wish the motion to be considered. The other options do not create an exception to the second requirement.

Reading and approval of the minutes.question: According to parliamentary procedure debate rules, all of the following are proper conduct EXCEPT
options: Members must address their remarks to the chair rather than directly to other members during debate
A member may speak against their own motion if they wish to change their position before the vote
A member may make two speeches on the same motion during a single meeting, with each speech limited to ten minutes
No member may speak a second time on a motion while another member who has not yet spoken is seeking the floor
Reading and approval of the minutes.explanation: The maker of a motion has the right to speak first in debate but may NOT speak against their own motion—they retain the right to vote against it, but not to argue against it during debate. This is a core rule protecting the mover's standing. The other three options correctly describe proper debate conduct: members are limited to two speeches of ten minutes each per motion per day, must address the chair rather than each other, and must yield the floor to members who have not yet spoken.

Reports of officers, boards, standing co.question: When a member wishes to bring a motion before the assembly, they must rise, address the chair, and receive recognition. Once the chair recognizes them, that member ___.
options: should address other members directly to build support
may immediately call for a vote on their proposed motion
must wait for another member to second before speaking
has the floor and may state their motion to the assembly
Reports of officers, boards, standing co.explanation: Having the floor means the member has the right to speak and present their motion. The second requirement (another member's second) comes after the motion is stated, not before. Addressing the chair and receiving recognition grants the floor; only then does the member state the motion.

Reports of officers, boards, standing co.question: In parliamentary procedure, the proper form for introducing a motion is ___.
options: I so move that the assembly accepts this idea
I move that the treasury be audited before year-end
I hereby propose that we take the following action
I make a motion that we adopt this proposal
Reports of officers, boards, standing co.explanation: The correct form begins with 'I move that' followed by the specific action. Saying 'I make a motion that' or 'I so move' are improper forms that do not meet parliamentary procedure standards.

Reports of officers, boards, standing co.question: Which of the following best describes the role of the presiding officer during debate on a pending motion?
options: The presiding officer is permitted to vote twice—once as a member and once to break a tie
The presiding officer may speak in favor of motions they personally support while maintaining the chair
The presiding officer decides which members may speak and for how long based on the merit of their arguments
The presiding officer must remain impartial and relinquish the chair if they wish to participate in debate
Reports of officers, boards, standing co.explanation: The presiding officer must be impartial during debate. If they wish to speak on the motion, they must step down from the chair and let another member preside. The chair does not return until the question is disposed of. This rule ensures the presiding officer does not use their position to influence debate.

Reports of officers, boards, standing co.question: A committee report is presented at the order-of-business stage 'Reports of officers, boards, standing committees.' A member then moves to adopt the committee's recommendation. Which of the following is true of this motion?
options: The motion must receive a two-thirds vote regardless of the committee's recommendation
The motion is automatically adopted without a vote if no member objects within five minutes
The motion does not require a second because it is committee-reported business
The motion requires a second from another member before the chair can state the question
Reports of officers, boards, standing co.explanation: Committee-reported motions are exempt from the requirement of a second. This is a specific exception in parliamentary procedure that recognizes the committee's internal deliberation process. All other motions require a second to come before the assembly.

Reports of officers, boards, standing co.question: In parliamentary procedure, a motion reported from a committee differs from a motion made from the floor in that a committee-reported motion EXCEPT
options: requires a second before the chair states the question
does not need a second in order to be considered by the assembly
comes before the assembly during the 'Reports of officers, boards, standing committees' stage
may be amended by members after being presented
Reports of officers, boards, standing co.explanation: Committee-reported motions do not require a second—this is a core procedural rule that distinguishes them from floor motions. All other options are true: they appear during reports, can be amended, and specifically do not need seconds. The key distinction is that a second is NOT required, making 'requires a second' the false statement.

Reports of officers, boards, standing co.question: A member raises their hand during a meeting, but before the chair can recognize them, another member who already has the floor is still speaking. Which of the following must occur before the waiting member can speak?
options: The current speaker must finish their allotted time or voluntarily relinquish the floor
The chair must call for a vote to remove the current speaker
The waiting member must second a motion from the current speaker
The current speaker must yield the floor and sit down
Reports of officers, boards, standing co.explanation: Only the member who has the floor may speak; the chair will not recognize a waiting member until the current speaker finishes their remarks or yields. The waiting member cannot interrupt, demand a vote, or second a statement—they must wait for the speaker to complete their allotted two speeches of ten minutes each.

Reports of officers, boards, standing co.question: The requirement that a member's speech must relate directly to the motion under debate, be directed to the chair rather than other members, and avoid personal attacks on motives is part of ___.
options: recognition protocol
the order of business
debate rules
the voting procedure
Reports of officers, boards, standing co.explanation: Debate rules in RONR establish standards for germane, respectful discourse during deliberation. These rules include the requirement to address the chair, stay on topic, refer to members impersonally, and avoid attacks on motives. The other options govern different aspects of the meeting structure.

Reports of officers, boards, standing co.question: Which of the following describes a voting method that is required to be used in order to verify a voice vote on demand or to decide any motion requiring a two-thirds majority?
options: Unanimous consent, which requires the absence of objections
Ballot voting, which allows members to cast secret votes
Roll call voting, which is used in all representative bodies
Rising vote, in which members stand to be counted
Reports of officers, boards, standing co.explanation: A rising vote—where members stand to be counted—is the method mandated when a voice vote result is challenged and must be verified, and it is also the required method for any motion requiring a two-thirds majority. Ballot voting is for secrecy (when bylaws require it), roll call records individual votes in representative bodies, and unanimous consent is a shortcut that can be used only if no member objects.

Reports of officers, boards, standing co.question: During a meeting following the standard order of business, a member wishes to raise a question about the treasurer's report that was just presented. At what point in the meeting could this member bring a motion to request an audit of the treasury accounts?
options: During Unfinished business and general orders, if the member has raised their hand first
Immediately after the Reports stage concludes and before Special orders begins
During the Reports of officers, boards, standing committees stage, before the treasurer finishes presenting
Only during the New business stage at the end of the agenda
Reports of officers, boards, standing co.explanation: A motion stemming from a committee or officer report belongs to the assembly immediately after that report is concluded and before the next order-of-business stage. The member does not have to wait until New business; the natural opening is the moment the Reports stage ends and the chair moves to Special orders. This allows the assembly to act on the reported business while it is fresh.

Reports of officers, boards, standing co.question: All of the following are proper debate rules in parliamentary procedure EXCEPT
options: The maker of a motion must speak against their own motion if they wish to participate in debate
A member may deliver up to two speeches on a motion, with each speech limited to ten minutes
No member may speak a second time on a motion while another member who has not yet spoken is seeking the floor
Debate must be germane to the motion and must be directed to the chair, not to other members
Reports of officers, boards, standing co.explanation: The maker of a motion has the right to speak first in debate but may NOT speak against their own motion—they may vote against it, but not argue against it orally. All other options correctly state debate rules: the two-speech limit, the requirement that debate be germane and directed to the chair, and the rule preventing a second speaker until all first-time speakers have been heard.

Reports of special (select) committees.question: In parliamentary procedure, the formal statement by the chair that repeats the motion and opens it for debate is called ___
options: putting the question
stating the question
seconding the motion
recognizing the member
Reports of special (select) committees.explanation: Stating the question is the chair's formal restatement of a motion (e.g., 'It is moved and seconded that...'), which signals that the motion now belongs to the assembly and debate may begin. This is distinct from putting the question, which means taking the vote after debate is closed.

Reports of special (select) committees.question: When a special committee presents its findings to the assembly, the motion to accept or implement the committee's recommendations is handled during which part of the standard order of business?
options: Unfinished business and general orders
New business
Reports of special (select) committees
Special orders
Reports of special (select) committees.explanation: Reports of special (select) committees is the designated point in the standard order of business when committees present their work and the assembly considers related motions. This occurs before unfinished business and allows the assembly to act on the committee's recommendations in their proper sequence.

Reports of special (select) committees.question: Which of the following would all allow a member to speak during debate on a main motion?
options: The member is speaking while another member who has spoken already seeks the floor, the member is addressing another member by name, and the member's remarks are not germane
The member is the presiding officer, the member is speaking about a matter of direct personal interest, and the member has not been recognized by the chair
The member has already spoken twice on this motion, the member is attacking the motives of the previous speaker, and the member is speaking for fifteen minutes
The member has not yet spoken on this motion today, the member was recognized by the chair, and the member's remarks are germane to the motion
Reports of special (select) committees.explanation: A member may speak only if they have not exhausted their two speeches per motion per day, have been recognized by the chair, and keep remarks germane to the motion. The other options each include violations of debate rules—exceeding speech limits, attacking motives, speaking without recognition, or speaking out of turn.

Reports of special (select) committees.question: A motion requires all of the following to come before the assembly for debate EXCEPT
options: recognition of the member by the chair
a formal statement of the motion by the chair
a second from another member
approval by the standing committee
Reports of special (select) committees.explanation: Standing committee approval is not required for a main motion to come before the assembly. The essential steps are member recognition, a proper motion statement, a second, and the chair's formal restatement (stating the question). Standing committees report their work, but their approval is not a prerequisite for members to make motions.

Reports of special (select) committees.question: In parliamentary procedure, when a committee reports its findings to the assembly, its recommendations must receive a second before debate can proceed on the proposed motion.
options: True — only if the committee is a standing committee, not a special committee
False — motions arising from committee reports do not require a second
True — all motions require a second before the chair can state the question
False — the chair states the question regardless of whether a second is given
Reports of special (select) committees.explanation: Committee-reported motions are an explicit exception to the general seconding rule. The source material states that committee reports come with built-in assembly support and do not need a second before debate begins. This distinction is critical for understanding how special committees function within the parliamentary process.

Reports of special (select) committees.question: For a main motion to come before the assembly for debate and voting, all of the following must occur EXCEPT
options: A member rises and addresses the chair
The chair recognizes the member and gives them the floor
The maker of the motion must speak against it during debate
Another member seconds the motion to show support
Reports of special (select) committees.explanation: The maker of a motion has the right to speak first in debate, but may never speak against their own motion—in fact, they typically speak in favor of it. The other three steps (rising and addressing the chair, receiving recognition, and obtaining a second) are all essential for a main motion to come before the assembly.

Reports of special (select) committees.question: Which of the following statements correctly describes the debate rules under parliamentary procedure?
options: Debate remarks must be directed to the chair and remain germane to the motion; personal attacks on members are not permitted
The presiding officer may enter debate on any motion while still presiding over the meeting
Members may speak in any order they choose; the chair does not control who has the floor
A member may give up to four speeches on the same motion in one day, each up to fifteen minutes long
Reports of special (select) committees.explanation: Debate must be germane, remarks must be addressed to the chair (not directly to other members), and members may not attack motives or refer to members by name. The actual limit is two speeches per member per motion per day, each up to ten minutes; the chair must remain impartial and does not participate in debate.

Reports of special (select) committees.question: The process by which a member initially brings a proposed action to the attention of the assembly—beginning with rising to address the chair and ending when the chair formally restates it—is called ___
options: voting on the question
a main motion
recognizing the floor
debating a motion
Reports of special (select) committees.explanation: A main motion is the formal process encompassing all six steps from a member's rise through the chair's statement of the question. The term describes the entire procedure by which a member brings business before the assembly, not just the debate or voting that follows.

Reports of special (select) committees.question: In parliamentary procedure, a motion reported by a special committee differs from a main motion made from the floor in that a committee-reported motion requires ___
options: the committee chair to present it in a formal written report
approval by the standing committee before it can be debated
no second to come before the assembly
a two-thirds vote instead of a simple majority to pass
Reports of special (select) committees.explanation: Committee-reported motions are exempt from the normal requirement that a main motion be seconded before debate can proceed. This exception recognizes that the committee itself has already vetted and authorized the motion, so a second from the floor is unnecessary. All other options misstate procedural rules for committee reports.

Reports of special (select) committees.question: Under parliamentary procedure, the proper form for a member to introduce a motion to the assembly is to say ___
options: Second, I move that we adopt the following resolution
I so move that this matter be brought before the body
I make a motion that the assembly approve this proposal
I move that we proceed with the following action
Reports of special (select) committees.explanation: The correct phrasing for a main motion is 'I move that...' — this is the formal language required by parliamentary procedure. The phrases 'I make a motion that,' 'I so move,' and improperly including a second in the motion itself are all improper forms that do not meet the standard for introducing a motion.

Special orders (matters set for this mee.question: A member who wishes to bring a proposal before the assembly must begin by stating the motion in what specific form?
options: I move that ...
I so move to bring this matter forward
I would like to propose that we take action on this item
I make a motion that the assembly consider this proposal
Special orders (matters set for this mee.explanation: Parliamentary procedure requires the exact phrasing 'I move that ...' to formally introduce a motion. Other phrasings like 'I make a motion that' or 'I so move' are improper forms and do not correctly state the motion under RONR rules.

Special orders (matters set for this mee.question: Which of the following actions would ALL be necessary for a main motion to properly come before the assembly?
options: A member rises and is recognized by the chair, states the motion, and the chair immediately puts it to a vote
A member is recognized, the motion is stated, the chair recognizes a second speaker, and debate begins
A member rises and is recognized, states the motion in proper form, another member seconds it, and the chair states the question
A member addresses the chair, makes a motion, the assembly discusses it, and the chair announces the result
Special orders (matters set for this mee.explanation: All six steps must occur in sequence: a member rises and is recognized, states the motion properly, receives a second, the chair states the question, debate occurs (if applicable), and then the chair puts the question to a vote. The correct option includes the critical steps of recognition, stating, seconding, and the chair's statement of the question.

Special orders (matters set for this mee.question: In debate, the member who originally made the motion has what special right that other members do not possess?
options: The right to prevent other members from speaking against the motion
The right to speak first in debate, though they may not speak against their own motion
The right to speak three times on the motion instead of the normal limit of two
The right to speak first in debate and to extend their speech beyond the ten-minute limit
Special orders (matters set for this mee.explanation: The maker of a motion has the privileged right to speak first during debate, but this privilege includes an important restriction: they may not speak against (oppose) their own motion, though they may still vote against it. This balances their advantage with fair debate procedure.

Special orders (matters set for this mee.question: The procedures that govern when a motion comes before the assembly, who may debate it, and how it is finally decided make up the process of ___.
options: parliamentary order
running business
motions and voting
conducting a meeting
Special orders (matters set for this mee.explanation: The entire sequence from a member's rise through debate, voting, and announcement of results is collectively the process of conducting a meeting or running business—the core function of parliamentary procedure. This encompasses recognition, stating the motion, seconding, stating the question, debate, voting, and announcement.

Special orders (matters set for this mee.question: The standard order of business includes all of the following EXCEPT
options: Opening ceremonies as the required first item after call to order
Reading and approval of the minutes as the third item
New business presented before announcements and adjournment
Special orders placed immediately after reports of special committees
Special orders (matters set for this mee.explanation: Opening ceremonies (such as a pledge or creed) are explicitly listed as optional in the standard order of business, not required. They occur after the call to order only if the organization chooses to include them. All other options correctly describe items in the standard ten-step order.

Special orders (matters set for this mee.question: The number of speeches a member is allowed to make on a single motion during one meeting is ___.
options: three
two
one
unlimited
Special orders (matters set for this mee.explanation: Under RONR rules, each member is limited to two speeches per motion per day, with a maximum of ten minutes per speech. This limit ensures fair participation and prevents any single member from dominating debate. The limit can only be changed by a motion to limit or extend debate, which requires a two-thirds vote.

Special orders (matters set for this mee.question: All of the following are correct statements about voting procedures EXCEPT ___.
options: A member may abstain from voting, and abstentions are counted as votes cast for determining the majority
If a member's vote would break or make a tie, the chair may vote even if the chair is a member
A majority vote requires more than half of the votes cast by members entitled to vote
On an appeal, a tie vote sustains the chair's decision rather than defeating the motion
Special orders (matters set for this mee.explanation: Abstentions are NOT counted as votes cast; only actual votes for and against are counted when determining whether a majority (or two-thirds) has been achieved. All other statements are correct: majority is more than half of votes cast, the chair's vote breaks or makes ties when necessary, and ties on appeals sustain the chair. This distinction is important for accurate vote counting.

Special orders (matters set for this mee.question: During debate on a main motion, all members may speak EXCEPT ___.
options: the presiding officer, who must remain impartial while chairing
members who have already used both of their two allowed speeches
members who have abstained from voting
members whose personal or pecuniary interest in the motion differs from other members
Special orders (matters set for this mee.explanation: Abstention during voting does not prevent a member from speaking in debate beforehand; abstention is a choice made at the time of voting, not a restriction on debate participation. The other options are genuine restrictions: members are limited to two speeches, the chair must not debate while presiding, and members with a direct personal or pecuniary interest not shared by others are barred from voting (though the source focuses on voting, this principle extends to debate participation for conflicted members). Abstaining from voting does not disqualify someone from earlier debate.

Special orders (matters set for this mee.question: In the standard order of business, at what point does the assembly address matters that were postponed from a previous meeting and set aside as priority items for this meeting?
options: During unfinished business and general orders, after reports are completed
During the reading and approval of minutes, if they are mentioned there
During new business, so members can decide whether to revisit them
During special orders, which comes before unfinished business
Special orders (matters set for this mee.explanation: Special orders are matters—including postponed items—that have been set for this specific meeting and take priority over unfinished business. They appear as item 6 in the standard order of business, after committee reports and before unfinished business. This allows the assembly to address pre-planned, high-priority items in their designated slot.

Special orders (matters set for this mee.question: When a member wishes to participate in debate on a main motion, what is the first action they must take to gain the right to speak?
options: State their position clearly so other members know where they stand
Rise and seek recognition from the chair
Submit a written statement of their views to the secretary
Call out 'Second!' to show their support for discussion
Special orders (matters set for this mee.explanation: A member must first rise and address the chair to be recognized; only the member recognized by the chair may speak. Once the chair recognizes them, they 'have the floor' and may then participate in debate. Without this recognition from the presiding officer, a member has no right to speak.

Unfinished business and general orders.question: The proper form for a member to introduce a motion is 'I move that...' What is the term for the action when another member says 'Second!' in response?
options: Ratification
Confirmation
Validation
Seconding
Unfinished business and general orders.explanation: A second shows that at least two members want the motion considered by the assembly. Without a second, the motion is simply not taken up before the assembly—it does not come before the body for debate or vote.

Unfinished business and general orders.question: The presiding officer's role during debate on a motion is to ___.
options: remain impartial and not enter the debate while presiding
prevent any member from speaking against the original motion
limit debate to only the first five minutes of discussion
vote on every motion to guide the assembly's decision
Unfinished business and general orders.explanation: RONR requires the presiding officer to maintain impartiality and must relinquish the chair if they wish to debate. This ensures fair and unbiased leadership during deliberation on motions.

Unfinished business and general orders.question: Which of the following correctly describes the relationship between unfinished business and the chair's role in bringing it before the assembly?
options: The chair must postpone all unfinished business until the next scheduled meeting to allow time for new business
The chair calls for unfinished business by asking members to identify pending items from the last meeting
Unfinished business comes up automatically from the previous meeting and is not 'called for' by the chair asking members
Members may object to unfinished business being brought up, which removes it from consideration entirely
Unfinished business and general orders.explanation: Unfinished business appears automatically in the standard order of business from the previous meeting; the chair does not solicit it by asking members. The term 'unfinished business' is also the correct parliamentary name—not 'old business.'

Unfinished business and general orders.question: The standard order of business includes reports of officers, boards, and standing committees. Which one of these steps does NOT occur in the order stated in a standard meeting agenda?
options: Opening ceremonies before the reading and approval of minutes
Announcements before the final adjournment
Special orders before unfinished business and general orders
New business immediately after reports of special committees
Unfinished business and general orders.explanation: New business comes after unfinished business and general orders, not immediately after special committee reports. The correct sequence places special orders (step 6) before unfinished business (step 7), and new business (step 8) follows unfinished business.

Unfinished business and general orders.question: Which of the following would ALL be proper debate procedures under RONR?
options: Members speak directly to other members rather than the chair, the presiding officer votes whenever present, and debate limits require unanimous consent to change
Members may attack the motives of previous speakers, speak a second time while others seek the floor, and refer to members by name
Members address remarks to the chair, keep debate germane to the motion, and may speak twice per motion per day
The presiding officer enters debate to clarify facts, members may speak three times per motion, and debate need not be germane
Unfinished business and general orders.explanation: Under RONR debate rules, members must address the chair (not other members), keep remarks germane to the motion, and are limited to two speeches per member per motion per day—each up to ten minutes. No member may speak a second time while another who hasn't spoken seeks the floor, motives may not be attacked, and the presiding officer must remain impartial and not debate while chairing. The correct answer bundles three genuinely required debate practices; the wrong options each contain specific procedure violations that prepared students would recognize.

Unfinished business and general orders.question: In the six steps of a main motion, the action that occurs after a member states the motion and another member responds 'Second!' is that the chair must ___.
options: state the question to bring the motion before the assembly
announce whether the motion has been properly seconded
recognize the member to begin debate immediately
take a vote on whether to accept the motion
Unfinished business and general orders.explanation: According to the six steps of a main motion, after the motion is stated and seconded, step 5 requires the chair to state the question—restating the motion in formal language like 'It is moved and seconded that...'—which officially brings it before the assembly. Only after the question is stated does debate (if debatable) proceed to the vote.

Unfinished business and general orders.question: A member who makes a motion has the right to speak first during debate on that motion. What is one key constraint on how that member may participate in the debate?
options: They may speak once, for up to five minutes total
They may vote against their own motion but cannot speak against it
They may not yield the floor to any other member under any circumstance
They must remain standing at the podium for the entire debate period
Unfinished business and general orders.explanation: The source material explicitly states that the maker of the motion has the right to speak first in debate but may not speak against their own motion, though they are permitted to vote against it. This unusual rule reflects the presumption that the member who introduced it initially favors it, yet allows a change of mind in the voting booth.

Unfinished business and general orders.question: Which of the following would ALL be proper requirements or restrictions on debate under RONR?
options: Debate must be germane to the motion, remarks must be addressed to the chair rather than other members, and no member may attack the motives of another
Members must refer to other members by their formal titles, no objections are permitted during debate, and the chair may vote on all motions while presiding
All members must speak in alphabetical order, debates may extend beyond ten minutes with unanimous consent, and the chair may participate in debate without relinquishing the gavel
Remarks must be addressed to the chair, debate must relate to the motion, and members may speak a maximum of three times per motion per day
Unfinished business and general orders.explanation: The source material specifies that debate must be germane (relevant to the motion), remarks must be addressed to the chair and not to other members directly, and no attacking of motives is permitted. Option A incorrectly states three times per day (correct is two). Option B incorrectly allows the chair to vote while presiding and prohibits objections. Option D incorrectly suggests the chair can debate while presiding.

Unfinished business and general orders.question: The term for the voting method in which the presiding officer repeats something like 'If there is no objection, the minutes are approved' and proceeds only if at least one member objects is ___.
options: show of hands
unanimous consent or general consent
rising vote
roll call vote
Unfinished business and general orders.explanation: The source material identifies this voting method as unanimous consent or general consent, in which the chair assumes agreement unless a single objection is raised. Any single objection forces a regular vote to be taken instead, ensuring that agreement is truly unanimous before adoption.

Unfinished business and general orders.question: Which of the following would ALL be correct statements about the standard order of business in a meeting?
options: The reading of minutes happens before officer reports, and unfinished business is automatically called for by the chair at the start of that section.
Opening ceremonies come before the reading and approval of minutes, and special orders are considered before unfinished business.
Reports of officers and standing committees are heard before reports of special committees, and new business follows unfinished business.
Announcements occur after new business, and adjournment is always the final item on the agenda.
Unfinished business and general orders.explanation: This option correctly sequences three true elements of the standard order of business: officer/standing committee reports before special committee reports (step 4 before step 5), and new business after unfinished business (step 8 after step 7). Option A is wrong because special orders (step 6) come after unfinished business (step 7), not before. Option C is incorrect because adjournment is optional and may not occur if the meeting concludes early. Option D misrepresents unfinished business by suggesting the chair calls for it, when in fact it comes up automatically from the previous meeting.

New business.question: When a member wishes to bring a motion before the assembly, the proper form is to say:
options: I so move that the assembly consider this matter
I move so that this motion is brought before the assembly
I make a motion that we proceed with this action
I move that ...
New business.explanation: The correct parliamentary form for stating a motion is 'I move that...' Phrases like 'I so move,' 'I make a motion that,' or other variations are improper forms and do not follow parliamentary procedure rules.

New business.question: The formal process by which a member obtains permission to speak, the motion is stated by the chair, and the assembly prepares to deliberate on a proposal is called ___.
options: Putting the question
Recognizing the member
Seconding the motion
Stating the question
New business.explanation: Stating the question is the moment when the chair formally restates the motion in the form 'It is moved and seconded that...' This makes the motion belong to the assembly and ready for debate. The other steps (recognition, seconding) come before this; putting the question comes after debate ends.

New business.question: Which of the following statements correctly describe how debate operates under Robert's Rules of Order?
options: Any member may speak twice per motion per day for up to ten minutes each time, and the maker of the motion may speak last
Members may address other members directly by name and must follow any time limits set by majority vote before debate begins
Only members recognized by the chair may speak, debate must be germane to the motion, and members must address the chair rather than other members directly
A member making the motion has the right to speak first in debate and may speak a second time against their own motion if time permits
New business.explanation: Under RONR, only recognized members may speak, debate must be germane, and speakers address the chair, not other members. Members are limited to two speeches per motion per day at ten minutes each; the maker speaks first but cannot speak against their own motion; and time limits require a 2/3 vote to change, not a simple majority decision beforehand.

New business.question: In a meeting with 23 members present, a main motion receives 14 votes in favor and 9 votes opposed. What is the status of the motion?
options: The motion is defeated because a tie would have broken the result
The motion is adopted because 14 votes is more than half of the 23 votes cast
The motion is adopted because 14 is more than half of the 23 members present
The motion is defeated because 14 votes do not equal a two-thirds majority
New business.explanation: A majority vote requires more than half of the votes CAST—not more than half of members present. With 23 votes cast (14 + 9), a majority is 12 votes. Since 14 exceeds 12, the motion is adopted. Only votes actually cast count; absent members are not included in the calculation.

New business.question: All of the following are proper voting methods under Robert's Rules of Order EXCEPT
options: Proxy voting by absent members whenever a quorum is present
Voice vote, which is the standard method for most majority votes
Ballot voting when the bylaws require secrecy in elections
Roll call voting in representative bodies to record each member's individual vote
New business.explanation: Proxy voting is NOT permitted under standard RONR unless the bylaws specifically authorize it. Voice, rising, ballot, roll call, and unanimous consent are all recognized voting methods. The RONR defaults allow only absentee/mail/proxy voting if bylaws expressly permit it—otherwise absent members simply do not vote.

New business.question: A second to a main motion indicates that ___.
options: the member making the motion has the right to speak first
the chair has officially recognized the motion
at least one other member wishes the motion to be considered
the motion will automatically pass without debate
New business.explanation: A second shows that at least two members want the motion brought before the assembly for deliberation. Without a second, the motion is simply not taken up—it does not come before the assembly at all. This is a procedural requirement that distinguishes a motion worthy of debate from one lacking support.

New business.question: Which of the following correctly describes requirements for debate under Robert's Rules of Order?
options: A member may speak a third time on the same motion if no other member has requested the floor
A member may speak in favor of a motion they did not propose, but must yield immediately if challenged
The presiding officer may participate in debate without leaving the chair
Members must direct all remarks to the chair and avoid personal attacks on other members
New business.explanation: Robert's Rules requires that debate remarks be directed to the chair (not other members directly), remain germane to the motion, and never attack members' motives—members are referred to impersonally. The other options violate core debate rules: members are limited to two speeches per motion per day, the presiding officer must relinquish the chair to debate, and members cannot speak against their own motion.

New business.question: In running a meeting, the order in which items are addressed follows the standard order of business. New business comes ___.
options: immediately after the approval of minutes and before officer reports
after unfinished business and general orders but before announcements
at the very beginning of the meeting to allow urgent matters priority
only if the chair decides that time permits before adjournment
New business.explanation: The standard order of business places new business after unfinished business and general orders (step 8) and before announcements (step 9). This ensures that items carried over from prior meetings or set as special orders are handled before entirely new proposals are introduced. New business is a fixed step in the agenda, not discretionary.

New business.question: Under Robert's Rules, all of the following are voting methods the assembly may use EXCEPT ___.
options: weighted voting, where each member's vote counts according to their position or membership level
roll call, which records each member's individual vote for transparency in representative bodies
general consent, where the chair announces a proposal and a single objection requires a formal vote
rising vote, which must be used to verify a voice vote on demand and is required for two-thirds motions
New business.explanation: Weighted voting is not a standard method under Robert's Rules of Order. The four legitimate voting methods are voice, rising, show of hands, ballot, roll call, unanimous/general consent, and mail/proxy (if bylaws permit). Weighted voting contradicts the principle of equal member standing. The other three options correctly describe actual RONR voting methods and their proper uses.

New business.question: All of the following are steps in the formal process of bringing a main motion before the assembly EXCEPT
options: A second member indicates support by saying 'Second'
Another member immediately votes on whether to consider the motion
A member rises and addresses the chair to obtain recognition
The chair states the question by repeating the motion to the assembly
New business.explanation: The correct six-step process requires a member to rise and be recognized, state the motion, receive a second, have the chair state the question, and then proceed to debate and voting — never an immediate vote before the question is stated. Voting occurs only after debate (if the motion is debatable) and the chair puts the question; a premature vote is not part of the procedure and would violate the assembly's right to debate.

Announcements.question: The point at which the chair formally addresses the assembly with the exact wording of the motion, bringing it officially before the members for consideration, is called ___.
options: stating the question
putting the question
seconding the motion
recognizing the member
Announcements.explanation: Stating the question is the chair's formal restatement of the motion (e.g., 'It is moved and seconded that ...'), which marks the moment the motion officially belongs to the assembly and becomes debatable. Recognizing the member comes earlier (giving them the floor), seconding requires another member's support, and putting the question refers to taking the vote.

Announcements.question: The process by which debate is conducted according to parliamentary rules, including limits on speeches and requirements for germane remarks, is collectively known as ___.
options: voting procedure
the order of business
motion recognition
debate protocol
Announcements.explanation: Debate protocol encompasses the rules governing discussion: only recognized speakers may speak, members may speak twice per motion (ten minutes each), remarks must be germane, and attacks on motives are forbidden. Voting procedure refers to methods of casting votes, motion recognition is the chair acknowledging a member, and the order of business is the sequence of meeting items.

Announcements.question: Which of the following statements about voting in parliamentary procedure is correct?
options: Members who abstain from voting are counted as voting against the motion
A majority vote requires more than half of all members present, regardless of whether they cast a ballot
The chair, if a member, may vote whenever their vote would change the outcome of a motion
A tie vote on a motion is broken by the chair casting a second vote to ensure a decision
Announcements.explanation: The chair votes only in specific circumstances: when the vote is by ballot, or when their vote would make or break a tie—this ensures the chair is not voting twice and maintains impartiality. A tie defeats the motion (no exception except on appeals). A majority is more than half of votes *cast*, not all members present; abstentions are not counted as votes and do not compel members.

Announcements.question: In parliamentary procedure, a motion that receives no second is said to ___.
options: be tabled until a second member agrees to support it
not come before the assembly and is simply not taken up
die on the floor and is removed from consideration
remain pending until the next meeting when it may be reintroduced
Announcements.explanation: According to RONR, a motion without a second does not come before the assembly—it is not considered 'dead on the floor' but rather is never formally brought to the membership for debate or vote. This distinguishes an unsupported motion from one that has been properly seconded and is now part of the assembly's business.

Announcements.question: The formal procedure by which members propose business, speak in turn under recognized rules, and reach a decision through voting comprises ___.
options: the parliamentary motion process
the order of business
a motion and its disposition
the standard agenda sequence
Announcements.explanation: The parliamentary motion process encompasses the six steps of a main motion—from a member rising and being recognized, through stating the motion, securing a second, having the chair state the question, conducting debate under established rules, and finally taking and announcing the vote. This integrated procedure is how an assembly formally conducts its business.

Announcements.question: In parliamentary procedure, the second to a motion primarily serves to demonstrate what?
options: That the motion has already been approved by the standing committee
That the presiding officer agrees with the substance of the proposal
That at least two members wish to have the motion considered by the assembly
That the member making the motion has sufficient support from the chair
Announcements.explanation: A second shows that at least two members want the motion taken up; without it, the motion simply does not come before the assembly. This is a procedural requirement, not an endorsement of the motion's content.

Announcements.question: All of the following are proper debate rules in parliamentary procedure EXCEPT
options: Debate must be germane to the question, and members must address the chair, not each other
A member may make up to two speeches per motion per day, with a ten-minute limit on each
The member who made the motion may speak first in debate and may vote against their own motion
The presiding officer may participate in debate while presiding if they remain impartial and allow other speakers first
Announcements.explanation: The presiding officer must relinquish the chair entirely to participate in debate and cannot return until the question is disposed of—they cannot debate while presiding. The other three statements are all accurate debate rules.

Announcements.question: Which of the following voting scenarios correctly applies parliamentary procedure rules?
options: The presiding officer votes twice—once during the regular vote and again to break a tie if needed
When 25 votes are cast, a majority is 12 votes, and a two-thirds vote requires at least 17 votes
When 30 votes are cast, a tie vote sustains a motion on any matter brought before the assembly
A member with a direct financial interest in a question may abstain, but only after attempting to vote once
Announcements.explanation: With 25 votes cast, a majority is more than half (13 or more, so 12 does not work—actually 13 is needed, but checking the math: 25 votes means majority is 13; however, the question states 12, which is incorrect). Actually, the correct statement is: 25 votes cast means majority = 13, two-thirds = at least 17 (rounding 16.67 up). Let me recalculate: the question option says majority is 12 and two-thirds is 17. For 25 votes, majority should be 13 and two-thirds should be 17. This option is flawed. Let me revise the entire question.

Announcements.question: The process of determining the winner of a vote may take different forms depending on the situation and the organization's rules. Which of the following accurately describes when specific voting methods are required or preferred in parliamentary procedure?
options: Ballot voting is always mandatory in every organization, while roll-call voting is only used in very large assemblies
Show of hands is the required method in all groups, and voice votes are used as a backup only when members cannot see one another
Voice votes are used for all majority decisions; rising votes are used only when a member demands verification or when a two-thirds vote is required
Unanimous consent may be used for routine matters, but any single objection requires that a formal ballot vote be taken immediately
Announcements.explanation: Voice vote (viva voce) is the normal method for majority votes, while rising votes are required to verify a voice vote result on demand and are always used for two-thirds votes. The other options misstate or oversimplify the proper use of voting methods.

Announcements.question: All of the following are proper steps in making a main motion EXCEPT
options: Another member seconds the motion to show at least two members want it considered
A member addresses the chair and waits to be recognized before speaking
The chair formally states the question before debate begins
The member states the motion using the phrase 'I make a motion that'
Announcements.explanation: The proper form is 'I move that...' — never 'I make a motion that.' The other three steps (rising to address the chair, obtaining a second, and the chair stating the question) are all correct parts of the six-step main motion process outlined in parliamentary procedure.

Adjournment (by motion, or by unanimous.question: The formal process by which a presiding officer repeats the motion to the assembly before debate begins is called ___.
options: Stating the question
Announcing the result
Seconding the motion
Recognizing the member
Adjournment (by motion, or by unanimous.explanation: Stating the question is the chair's formal restatement of the motion—'It is moved and seconded that...'—which officially brings the motion before the assembly for consideration. This step comes after a second is received and before any debate occurs. Recognition, seconding, and announcing the result are separate parts of parliamentary procedure that happen at different stages.

Adjournment (by motion, or by unanimous.question: All of the processes by which members express their preference on a motion, including voice votes, ballot votes, and roll-call votes, are collectively known as ___.
options: Order of business
Motion recognition
Voting methods
Debate procedure
Adjournment (by motion, or by unanimous.explanation: Voting methods encompass all the different ways an assembly can record and count member preferences—voice, rising, ballot, roll call, unanimous consent, and others outlined in parliamentary rules. Debate procedure concerns how members discuss motions; order of business is the sequence of meeting topics; and motion recognition is the chair's acknowledgment that a member may speak.

Adjournment (by motion, or by unanimous.question: When a motion receives no second, what happens to it?
options: The chair may second it on behalf of the assembly if the motion is urgent
It simply does not come before the assembly and is not taken up
It is debated anyway, since one member proposed it
It is declared dead on the floor and formally recorded as rejected
Adjournment (by motion, or by unanimous.explanation: A motion that receives no second simply does not come before the assembly—it is not formally taken up, debated, or voted on. The absence of a second is not a formal rejection; rather, it means the motion never enters the assembly's consideration. The terms 'dead on the floor' and the idea that the chair can supply a second are both incorrect under standard parliamentary procedure.

Adjournment (by motion, or by unanimous.question: What is the proper way for a member to introduce a motion to the assembly?
options: I move that we approve the budget.
I'd like to move that we approve the budget.
I make a motion that we approve the budget.
I so move that we approve the budget.
Adjournment (by motion, or by unanimous.explanation: The correct formal phrasing is 'I move that...' — never 'I make a motion that' or 'I so move,' both of which are improper forms in parliamentary procedure. This is the prescribed language in RONR that ensures the motion is stated clearly and correctly to the assembly.

Adjournment (by motion, or by unanimous.question: Which of the following would NOT be a required step in the standard six-step process for bringing a main motion before the assembly?
options: Another member must second the motion before the chair states the question
The chair announces the result and declares the motion adopted or defeated
A member rises and addresses the chair to gain recognition
The member proposing the motion must first obtain written approval from the presiding officer
Adjournment (by motion, or by unanimous.explanation: The six-step process requires recognition, a second, the chair stating the question, and a vote/announcement—but nowhere does it require the proposer to obtain the chair's written approval beforehand. Written approval is not part of RONR procedure for main motions; the chair's role is to recognize speakers and manage debate, not to pre-authorize motions. The other three options are all actual steps in the formal process.

Adjournment (by motion, or by unanimous.question: A member's right to speak first in debate on a motion they have proposed, and their obligation to refrain from speaking against their own motion, reflects which fundamental principle of parliamentary procedure?
options: The chair's authority to control all debate topics and speaker order
The mover's privilege and responsibility in initiating assembly business
The requirement that all members speak an equal number of times
The rule that debate must alternate between supporters and opponents
Adjournment (by motion, or by unanimous.explanation: The mover's privilege ensures the person who brings a motion has priority to open debate and set the tone, while their inability to speak against their own motion reflects accountability—they cannot argue both sides of their own proposal. This is a foundational rule in the six-step motion process, distinct from general chair authority or debate equality rules.

Adjournment (by motion, or by unanimous.question: What is the term for the presiding officer's announcement that repeats the motion back to the assembly and formally opens it for discussion and decision?
options: Seconding the motion
Recognizing the member
Putting the question
Calling the question
Adjournment (by motion, or by unanimous.explanation: Putting the question is the chair's formal action to take the vote after debate is complete. Note that stating the question (already asked in this pool) occurs earlier, before debate begins; putting it happens at debate's end. This is a standard procedural term that closes discussion and moves to voting.

Adjournment (by motion, or by unanimous.question: A second to a motion shows that ___.
options: The motion has met all requirements and debate may now begin immediately
The member who moved the motion has the right to speak first in debate
The chair has officially recognized the member as having the floor
At least two members believe the motion is worth the assembly's consideration
Adjournment (by motion, or by unanimous.explanation: A second demonstrates that at least one other member supports bringing the motion before the assembly—it is a check ensuring a motion is not frivolous. A second does not mean debate starts immediately (the chair must state the question first), does not grant floor recognition (that happens before the motion is stated), and does not guarantee speaking rights (the mover has first-speaker privilege, but the second itself doesn't grant or deny rights).

Adjournment (by motion, or by unanimous.question: In parliamentary procedure, the process by which a member informs the chair they wish to speak is called ___.
options: obtaining recognition
requesting debate
seeking the floor
addressing the chair
Adjournment (by motion, or by unanimous.explanation: A member obtains recognition by addressing the chair and waiting for the presiding officer to acknowledge them, formally granting them permission to speak. This is a foundational step that must occur before a member can state a motion or participate in debate, ensuring the chair maintains order by controlling who speaks and when.

Adjournment (by motion, or by unanimous.question: The fundamental assembly rule that allows business to proceed without formal votes when no member objects, such as when the chair says 'If there is no objection, we will approve the minutes as read,' represents which aspect of parliamentary voting?
options: Roll-call documentation
Unanimous consent
Rising vote verification
Ballot vote secrecy
Adjournment (by motion, or by unanimous.explanation: Unanimous consent (also called general consent) is a voting method that expedites assembly business by assuming agreement unless a single member objects, triggering a formal vote. This procedure balances efficiency with protection of minority rights, since any member's objection forces the assembly to conduct a regular vote instead.
